commands.note: WinDefend is STOPPED — Defender dormant. Kaspersky is the active defense.

commands.note: Standard user with DEBUG privilege — can attach to processes, read memory, set breakpoints.

commands.command:
# Dark Room — HWBP on EtwEventWrite
# DR0 = EtwEventWrite address
# DR7 = 0x101 (local + global enable, exact match)
commands.note: Zero memory writes. No patching. The breakpoint is hardware — invisible to software scanners.
Target: .92 Intel machine, Windows 11 Home Build 26200
Kaspersky active — two avp.exe processes. Real-world target.

commands.command:
# Blue team detection vectors:
# 1. Hypervisor-based tracing (Intel PT, AMD IBS)
# 2. Kernel callbacks on debug register access
# 3. ETW session analysis — look for gaps in event flow
# 4. Memory forensics — check CONTEXT structures for Dr7 != 0
commands.output:
Detection difficulty: HIGH

Hardware breakpoints leave no memory artifacts.
No API hooks. No patched bytes.
The only evidence is in the thread context —
and that's volatile (gone on thread exit).
commands.note: This is why Dark Room is dangerous. It subverts the logging mechanism itself.

commands.output:
1. Find EtwEventWrite in ntdll.dll
2. Allocate a thread in your target process
3. Set CONTEXT_DEBUG_REGISTERS with Dr0 = EtwEventWrite
4. Set Dr7 = 0x101 (local + global enable)
5. Call NtSetContextThread
6. Trigger an ETW event (e.g., PowerShell execution)
7. Verify: Event should NOT appear in Windows Event Log
8. BONUS: Detect your own bypass using memory forensics
commands.note: Test on .92 (Kaspersky active) or .42 (clean sandbox). Document what works and what doesn't.
